feedback.text: I will begin with something simple. I see myself as a Customer in the Digital Landscape first, a Consumer second. Since my mind defines "Consumer" as a person who "consumes a procured product in it's entirety" . I have been made aware of the opening of the Feedbacks to the Digital Fairness Act, due to the "Stop Killing Games" movement, which is a good cause, but also should not be the only focus here. I do want to add some small personal information, just to give a better understanding of other numbers i may use. Some would consider me a Gen X ( born sometime in the 1970s ) that considers PC gaming his main hobby since the 1990s and someone who considers the german constitution as a good guideline of how to act towards others and what should be considered legal or not. Now, to my experiences growing up with the changes of and in the Digital Landscape. Among the first games i played, was Civilization. And i would still be able to play it today, from the same disk ( if i still had compatible hardware around ) . But when the Publisher of Civilization 6 is gone or decides it's no longer profitable to host the server, I would no longer be able to install and play my physical copy of Civilization 6, because it needs an online verification during installation. yes, this is meant as a copy protection mechanism, i do understand that very well, but it still makes this game ( and many others ) unavailable to future generations. to compare it... Civ 1 is a book i can read whenever i want. But Civ 6 is a book that i can only read after i somehow verified to the books publisher that i really did buy that book, whenever i decide to pick it up again. This also applies to Software as a whole. In conclusion, a digital good, that is purchased on a physical medium, should still be usable, when the manufacturer ( developer and/or publisher ) are no longer around or no longer directly supports it ( hosting a server to verify installation ) . Some forms of Software may be viable or even essential in the future ( for example manufacturing or medical facilities ) , but would no longer be useable. I also played games, that are meant to be played with others and thus have no "single player mode" , so from my perspective and personal experience. It is acceptable for such games, to only be playable through a subscription, since hosting a server does cost money. It is acceptable for "free-to-play" games, to have shops and/or "microtransactions" ( some are even macro... ) to finance hosting their servers ( and make "some" profit... ) . I also understand and find it acceptable, when such games do have to shut down, once the cost of hosting the servers are higher then the income from subscriptions. what i deem unacceptable is, having both a subscription model and microtransactions, when it is not a "free-to-play" game ( even if it is, not a nice thing to see ) . speaking of microtransactions, they should not contain gamble mechanics, even for persons without addictions, it can be tempting to get that on nice looking item ( a digital good inside a digital good, how fascinating ) and blow past your budget without realizing it. I have yet to personally experience the grief of being unable to play old games or use certain software, but i do support preserving digital goods ( software / games ) and making them useable for/in future generations. Personally, i still somewhat question the legality of Game Publishers deleting already installed games from my game library, once they decide it is no longer profitable or want to promote the next new game of the franchise. Before i forget. even though it is a bit way to late... i question the legality of Companies / Corporations, retro-actively changing their TOS, especially when they use my data to train AI, to then create profit from it.
